great progress in sustaining the peace through development when this administration first came in we faced the twin problem of insecurity and ina- hu- inadequate infrastructure this was complicated by the challenge of dwindling revenue from an average of ten billion naira per month our income dropped to about three billion naira per month no government since the creation of our state had faced such a great challenge infrastructure provision of infrastructure we know is the foundation of development the state has embarked on various infrastructural development project to fast-track development in all facets of our social political and economic life the engine for industrial growth is electricity government has taken cautious steps to tackle the problem of electricity distribution in the state and tried to stabilise power supply as i speak to you we have purchased close to one thousand transformers in the next few months when shell petroleum development company concludes arrangements to supply uninterrupted gas to the state turbines residents of bayelsa state will have more reason to smile as there will be no more power outages when the administration came in a water king had been built in yenagoa but the reticulation of the water was not yet done within the first one hundred days of our administration we made pipe borne water to run for the first time in the state capital since the creation of bayelsa state a point of fact government embarked on fourteen water schemes and two major regional water schemes these are the nembe regional water scheme and the oloibiri regional water scheme on assumption of off- of office the administration immediately took steps to tackle the non-availability of internal roads in the state capital yenagoa to a large extent had the look of a rural village with almost no access roads available to residents promptly government embarked on the construction of the largest number of roads ever undertaken by any administration at once in the history of bayelsa and perhaps all of nigeria so far over half of these roads have been completed our huge investment in infrastructure is predicated on the belief that as a new state those who make the efforts to come in here to invest and contribute to development of the state should unavoidably enjoy basic amenities education we see education as central in our overall objective of attaining a secure peaceful and prosperous bayelsa we also believe that a new state like bayelsa which is considered educationally disadvantaged needs to strengthen its manpower base as a new state we understand we have to do a few things that older states have taken for granted government has continued the policy of paying registration fees for all bayelsa state indigenes in public schools sitting for the senior secondary school certificate examinations conducted by the west african examination council and the national examination council as well as the junior secondary school certificate examination in two thousand and nine the state came first in the neco examinations this was a significant departure from the awful last position the state took in two thousand and eight government has embarked on the construction of three model secondary schools one in each of the three senatorial districts in the state sited in nembe sagbama and yenagoa these model secondary schools are planned to be of international standard on completion also in full swing is the upgrade of two existing secondary schools in each of the eight local government areas government has undertaken massive renovation of schools in the state at the tertiary level government has approved the establishment of the bayelsa state college of education at okpoama i am happy to announce that this college will commence academic activities soon in addition government facilitated the establishment of the federal polytechnic at ?

university 's in the university beg your pardon have been visited by the national universities commission accreditation team the recent being the visit in december two thousand and eight to the programmes in the department of biological sciences which all returned full accreditation a total of twenty eight programmes have returned full accreditation status in all while eight have interim status referring to the need for a revisit not too long from now other programmes such as bsc and msc architecture bsc estate management bsc banking and finance bsc accounting beng electrical my dear countrymen and women today marks another important milestone in our nation 's political history as we commemorate our democracy day the significance of our celebration today lies in the fact that we are able as a nation to look at the journey which we have made so far and set for ourselves a worthy cause for the days ahead an occasion such as this should naturally therefore provoke wider reflection on our democratic enterprise it 's indeed a golden opportunity for introspection on our chequered journey to the democratic promised land this year being the fiftieth anniversary of our national independence will surely record more of this interrogation of our past as we earnestly look into the future with boldness and optimism within the last century the anti-colonial struggle was the greatest issue of democracy and justice universally our founding fathers discharged their historic responsibility by fighting and winning our national freedom today therefore it 's a day to appreciate our resilience our patriotism and our faith in this land of our common destiny it 's a day to remember as our national anthem clearly instruct us the labour of our heroes past so when we reflect on democracy we remember the struggles of our great nationalist we remember the herbert macaulays nnamdi azikiwes the obafemi awolowos the ahmadu bellos the ernest nkolis the aminu kanos the margaret ekpos the gambo sawabas and our living legend anthony enahoro we of course remember all the vibrant and youthful members of the zikist movement the second wave of the struggle for democracy was to put an end to military rule that paved the way for this dispensation that began on may twenty nine nineteen ninety nine it 's in this spirit that we remember the great sacrifices made by nigerian 's patriots some of who paid the supreme sacrifice we salute the illustrious memories of the m k o abiolas the shehu musa yar'aduas the alfred rewanes and the gani fawehinmis the sacrifices that our compatriots living and dead have made to enthrone democracy should compel us to take our democratic enterprise more seriously and on to greater vigilance we must work to ensure that by the supreme sacrifices of these great heroes it should not longer be necessary for the tree of freedom to be watered by the blood of yet more martyrs it is for this and many other reasons that our challenge in furtherance of democracy as a nation is to conduct credible elections in which every voter will only be entitled to a vote and every vote will count we must develop a democratic culture in which the will of the people will be treated as sacred and immune to subversion by anti-democratic elements that is why the consummation of the process of electoral reform is a collective task that must be done this year in this regard we must continue to encourage the national assembly so that they can finish the good work already begun and in good time it is also part of the democratic challenge before us that we must build our institutions for democracy the legislature the judiciary the civil society the media and others the armed forces security agencies and law enforcement organisations must continue to be reoriented in tune with this democratic culture the supremacy of the constitution should always be defended by all regardless of divergence of interests opposing views should always be tolerated and a culture of unfettered national debate should be re- i- invigorated as we collectively seek solutions to our problems it is when we do all the foregoing and much more that we can truly say that the nation is consolidating the gains of democracy and that our democratic enterprise is yielding acceptable dividends for a democracy to be viral it must be genuinely competitive and strictly rule-based therefore our political parties should be developed as vital institution of democracy we run a multiparty system and the parties should be fair competitors while performing their constitutional functions however for the parties to be relevant the nation 's democratic enterprise it 's compulsory that a regime of internal party democracy must prevail in the parties it is the political parties that are indeed a democratic spirit that will de- deepen the practice of democracy in our land as we march faithfully and boldly on this journey we must erm remind ourselves that it is not going to be easy democracy call for sacrifice and tolerance an open ear and a strong voice democracy requires constant nurturing and internal vigilance democracy gives no room to lethargy as we strive to advance our democratic development there will be time and our will shall be tested our patience provoked and our belief questioned we must condemn occasions for ?
the beauty of democracy is that its practice is never final and always has room for improvement no matter how old the democratic society may be where we falter we must not fall when we are weak we must not surrender the conduct of the affairs of government in policy legislation decision making in education keep transforming in a democratic society thus democracy is not only about elections but also about the degree of popular participation in daily governance as well as individual and group freedoms and erm liberties including the freedom of association the right to practise the chosen religion and equal protection under the law democracy should give us all the confidence we need as a people to conduct our personal and business affairs assured that we are protected under the constitution of nigeria regardless of what part of the country we reside and regardless of our religious or ethnic affiliation our first responsibility as citizen is to use the ballot for us servants in government it is a responsibility to ensure that all qualified citizens have access to the ballot and that all votes count let me once again assure all nigerians that this time under my watch all votes will count it is my sacred duty to ensure that you are able to vote and that your votes count it is important to know that electoral integrity is not only about electoral reforms and legal and constitutional changes but also about changes in attitude and conduct above all it is about exemplary leadership and principled followership i am convinced that nigeria will attain electoral integrity in two thousand and eleven and beyond my dear compatriots we have to win for our children democratic freedoms that guarantee economic and social dividends that in turn engender and strengthen our nationhood let it be said by the generation that is to come that we unified this country and consolidated our democracy finally this democracy day celebration will not be complete if i do not touch on workers ' welfare it is well known that this administration has never abdicated its responsibility to the nigerian worker indeed we support the right of every worker to earn a living wage for addressing basic necessities of life you will recall that at the last may day celebration we assured workers that we will address the problems of distortions in pay relativity in the public service this has now been resolved positively the new salary scale shall take effect from july one two thousand and ten also in our resolve to address the housing challenge of civil servant government has approved the release of the sum of ten billion naira to the federal government staff housing loan body for onward erm disbursement to eligible civil servant as loans to enable them own their own houses let me therefore charge workers to reciprocate government 's gesture by increasing their productivity levels and shunning acts capable of undermining the development agenda of government knowing fully well that they are the drivers of the administration 's policies their conduct and productivity shall henceforth be guided by performance management and their operational guidelines that are expected to be concluded not later than december thirty first two thousand and ten i wish all nigerians a happy democracy day may god bless our beloved country

impressive rate of between six and seven per cent annually what is clear however is that such growth has not led to real development unemployment and poverty have continued to deepen what we must seek wage of eighteen thousand naira was announced with a call on the government to quickly pass it into law unfortunately it has taken almost a year for the amendment to the minimum wage act to be passed now that the law has been passed we must commit to ensuring that the law is observed and that the new wage is implemented we have had some discordant notes that some of the state governors about it we receive sun light throughout the day for every day throughout the year we receive enough rainfall to make our soil fertile enough such that if one throws even a grain of maize anywhere in the country it will geminate all the natural disasters that create havoc and bring sorrow and grief to so many people in different countries of the world do not occur in our dear country earthquakes volcanic eruptions hurricanes wild fires destructive floods snow storms are not experienced in our beloved country we enjoy a unique location we are placed almost halfway between the east and the west the north and the south of africa indeed nigeria is at the centre of the african continent our population is so large that one out of every seven africans is a nigerian indeed we are truly the heart of africa even our natural endowments are so enormous we are one of the largest producers of cassava in the world some few years ago nigeria was the largest producer of palm oil in the whole world in the past our groundnut pyramids marked us out as a major and important producer of agricultural produce our coal reserves are huge the- there is hardly any major solid mineral that is not found in commercial quantities in our dear country our large coastline is washed by the atlantic ocean our dear nation is supplied fresh water from t- our two major rivers niger and benue with many of their as my people would say when it 's time for work you work today we are going back to the controversy over the proposed amendments to the electoral act twenty ten it 's instructive to provide some background here the electoral act twenty ten was signed into law on august nineteen twenty ten by president goodluck jonathan after the two chambers of the national assembly had passed their harmonised version of the bill it 's also instructive to know that even when the electoral law had not been operationalised certain innovative provisions in the law gave immediate hope of a better electoral regime better managed and better supervised in particular the new law contained provisions seen as bold attempts to deepen internal democracy in the political parties an apparent lesson learned from the unwholesome actions of the political parties in the countdown to the twenty to the two thousand and seven general elections worthy of mention here is the removal of the initial provision in the two thousand and six electoral act which allowed political parties to substitute candidates by adducing cogent and verifiable reasons for doing so which the parties particularly the people 's democratic party never obeyed anyway as exemplified in the cases of rotimi amaechi and senator ifeanyi araraume new law under -s the two twenty ten electoral act that clause was removed and once a candidate emerged through the process outlined in section eighty seven of the new act section thirty three makes it completely impossible for the party to withdraw or substitute such candidates the only exceptions to this however are if the candidate on his own volition wrote to inec to withdraw his nomination or where the candidate dies before election section eighty seven of the new electoral act is perhaps the most progressive it outlined a rigorous process of direct or indirect congresses through which a candidate can only emerge and by this undemocratic conducts of the past such as impositions consensus anointment and coronations of candidates became outlawed in signing the acts president jonathan had observed and let me quote him here directly although serious concerns have been raised regarding some of the se- erm sections and their capa- erm capacity to constitute our inspiration for expanding democratic space i am driven by the belief that the promise of those processes has greater potential and options to broaden and advance our march to democratic freedom rather than undermine it unquote now it appear that the president 's declaration will be put to test even before the ink dried on the paper less than four months and without the benefit of testing this new law erm practically two efforts have been launched to tinker with the law and both attempts have raised serious concerns whether truly our leaders actually mean it when they say they are committed to deepen democracy first effort to amend the electoral act was from the executive which proposed some new clauses and the deleting of some an act which political observers saw as an attempt to whittle down the effect of section eighty seven of the act on the procedure for the emergence of candidates apparently being sensitive to the mood of the public the senate promptly reject that proposal without any debate soon after another effort this time around sponsored and coordinated by some key members of the national assembly began the current proposed amendment basically aim at making every member of the national assembly an automatic member of the national executive committee of his or her political party is an elaborate proposal to again amend section eighty seven of the act the main arguments of the lawmakers has been that they want to deepen internal democracy in these parties by reducing the effect of power blocks so far preponderance of public opinion seems to run against these proposed amendments even as the lawmakers appear bent on achieving their plot on focus consecutive bills while the senate received twelve bills for concurrence from the house of representatives one hundred and thirty six bills were read for the first time thirty nine bills were read second time and referred to committees for further legislative action and five bills were withdrawn two bills were negated which is the technical and legislative word for rejection while six bills were not given even a first reading among the twelve bills passed include appropriation bills for the nation the federal capital territory and the nddc others include the hydroelectric power producing areas development commission bill two thousand and nine the national hostel act amendment bill two thousand and nine discrimination against persons with disabilities prohibition bill two thousand and nine and the state of the nation address bill two thousand and nine the attempt to begin the process of a review and amendment of the ninety nine constitution suffered an initial setback on january sixteenth two thousand and nine when forty four members of the house of representatives withdrew from a joint retreat for the review of the constitution in minna niger state in spite of the house members ' withdrawal the forty four member senate committee sat through the retreat and in october two thousand and nine in kaduna held yet another special retreat where they resolved to adopt an incremental approach to constitutional review essential issues for the proposed amendment and constitutional review were spelled out thoroughly discussed and analysed at the senate national public hearing held in abuja between the thirteenth and fifteenth october two thousand and nine they include land use amendment bill two thousand and nine and the electoral matters with special emphasis on the constitution and the second amendment bill two thousand and nine other issues include provisions for the independent candidature prohibition of cross-carpeting by elected persons before the expiration of their tenure funding of political parties and independent funding of the independent national electoral commission with the theme legislating for an enduring electoral system in nigeria the senate at its two thousand and nine retreat held in enugu between the f- tenth and fourteenth november two thousand and nine again examined old issues and challenges on the proposed constitutional amendment other renewed impetus for the proposed amendment the senate held zonal public hearings simultaneously at the six geopolitical zones between the fourteenth and fifteenth of december two thousand and nine with all the public hearings over what next in spite of the senator 's optimism some of the nigerians including particularly the media continue to remain sceptical on the success of the review exercise as the senate has reconvened plenary on january twelfth two thousand and ten top on their agenda would be the issue of constitutional review and the passage of the twenty ten four trillion naira budget senate house are expected to conclude the passage of the petroleum industry bill and also consider the reports of the senate ad hoc committees that investigated the immanent food crisis in nigeria and deplorable condition of nigeria 's transport sector

so i 've talked about things fall apart i 've talked about death and the king 's horseman in trying to let you know that when we are exploring literature we must know that literature is a mirror image of life literature is an imaginative recreation of lived experience alright so it is not totally divorced from life instead it holds up the mirror to life it refracts life it reflects life it mimics life it imitates life that 's what literature does and when you are using the archetypal approach you are supposed to look at all these dimensions that i have mentioned alright so you look at those ritual conflicts that you have you may look at a novel you see that the conflict is the one that is called sibling rivalry when you have brother versus brother fighting you can have generational conflicts for example read purple hibiscus you look at relation between jaja and the father you know the relationship between kambili and the mother you do n't just run off without looking at the importance of the name kambili let me leave and this is a novel that revolves around the search for personal freedom and you can see how the writer carefully chooses the name he gives to the heroine the protagonist of the novel so it 's important whatever novel you are gon na look at see whether there is any thematic implication in the name of the characters in the names of the characters you know and see whether there 's any bearing you look at all of that the name the setting and what have you okay so in erm what they call archetypal criticism we also look at the importance of myth the importance of myth the validity of myth alright i 've tol- spoken to you about erm collective unconscious the importance of myth you 're exploring myth the question is what is myth what is myth some people say myth is a false story about erm a people 's erm way of life or whatever myth is not particularly false myth might just be you know erm a a kind of either spiritual or cultural or ideological belief alright about the way things work the patterns of things you know and you realise that sometimes myth informs reality and vice versa that 's exactly what you find you know erm in myth alright so this theo- this theory or this approach also explores the universal patterns of man the universal patterns of man alright do n't forget that life is about birth death and rebirth okay birth death and rebirth i 'm sure some of you have read things fall apart you will see when somebody was being buried and everybody was singing and dancing achebe records that in igbo culture there is a commingling between the living the dead and the unborn okay that is why the dead among igbo people are not buried in burial grounds they bury them within the homestead when my father died nineteen seventy six my father was buried right inside our house a family house and they lowered his gra- his coffin into the grave and we 're all asked to throw coins into the grave and they covered up the grave and they took us by the hand alright i was eight at the time and we were havin- we were stepping on one another because children are supposed to bury their parents and not the other way round because the oracle says when the parent plantain dies the stalkers take their place that is the way of life so you see we did that so because of the fact that my father was buried within the homestead it is assumed that my father is still around he 's still with us that is the spirit is still with us alright so and if for example three months on a young boy is born there 's every likelihood they may say babatunde my father is back there 's a a cousin of mine for example whom everybody in the family reveres it is assumed that he is the reincarnated form of my grandfather anon himself that anon made a promise when he was alive that no that when he comes back to this world he 's not going to go to farm he 's going to read books gon na be intellectual and all of that so this cousin of mine you know he he he he did all of that he was was n't interested in going to farm was so intelligent he 's a lawyer you know and all of that and people see him as erm anon come back to life you know so you realise that children like that they are called iyabo babatunde in traditional society even modern times they are accorded a lot of what respect even if they are children okay that is it because we believe in birth death and rebirth because the cycle of life is what open ended and continual you get the point i 'm making it is also symbolic of nature dry gives way to what to wet season ad infinitum that is the way it is we do n't believe that death is a nullity that death is a finality that death is an existential cul-de-sac we believe that death is simply a staging house alright a halfway house where you change form and move to another plane of existence all of this you are supposed to see erm you know when you look at literature alright so you want to look at the universal patterns of of man you know vis-à-vis the myth of eternal return alright what you find in nature harmatan giving way to wet season and in the western world you talk about the four seasons of summer autumn and of course winter then spring so you are looking at all of that okay it 's so very important you have all of this at the back of your mind i am quickly going to give you erm for the little to one theme okay that now we want to look at some of the thematic concerns of things fall apart for example things fall apart looks at clash of cultures you 'll now begin to explore that in your own of national disasters has shown an upward trend over the last decade and has set back the economic and social growth of most developing countries this assertion was made at this year 's world meteorological day celebration held in ibadan recognising the fact that ninety per cent of all natural hazards are related to weather climate and water the world meteorological organisation chose as its theme for celebration preventing and mitigating natural disasters the secretary general for the organisation mister jarod in a message said preventing and mitigating natural disasters is a top priority for the organisation delivering the message the assistant general manager south west zone the nigerian meteorological agency mister olaoluwa ogunyemi observed that while natural hazards may not be avoided early warnings prevention and mitigating measures can prevent them from becoming disasters in his lecture titled weather correlated disasters and the human factors enhancing them the nigerian experience professor oluyemi oladiran observed that though some other natural disasters may result from long-term effects of land degradation deforestation erosions or industrial emanations they 're not always naturally induced professor oladiran said sometimes when normal weather occurs the environmental situation can generate disasters from natural means professor oladiran also suggested that practices such as mountain wind farming building of roads which shield water to drainages forestation backed by irrigation and controlled effluent of atmospheric materials should be issues of current national importance earlier chairman at the occasion professor bayo omotoso had called on governments to pay more attention to the weather for adequate food security mosun salubi nta another in nineteen forty seven and nineteen fifty nine the most recent was in two thousand and one tomorrow eclipse will occur between nine and ten in the morning and how prepared are nigerians for the eclipse experts warn that the eclipse should not be viewed directly with eyes it 's also not safe to watch it through water or use sun glasses professor emeritus faniran is from the geography department university of ibadan while doctor oluleye is from the university college hospital uch ibadan they advised those driving during tomorrow 's eclipse to put on their full light to be able to see properly segun lawole nta news over the years the expected rest after long service to one 's nation is not the case of pensioners in nigeria this is because the retirement benefits like gratuity and pension allowances have become a problem that has defied all solutions backlog of pensioner arrears of those who were supposed to be enjoying are not paid while some have subsequently lost their lives in order to overcome this the pension reform act which establishes the contributory pension scheme became a law in june two thousand and four the main objectives of the new contributory pension scheme are to ensure that every person who had worked either in public or private sector receives his or her retirement benefit as at when due and assisting employees to save to cater for their livelihood during old age also it is established to have a uniform set of rules and regulations for the administration and payment of pension funds in nigeria pension funds are to be managed by pension fund administrators pfas licensed by the national pension commission each employee is therefore mandated to choose any pension fund the administrator in order not to jeopardise the objectives of the new pension scheme there is the need to have deep knowledge about the various pfas it is against this background that the university of ibadan organised an interactive session for its staff and the licensed pfas the staff commended the initiative of the ui management the new scheme mandates employers and employees to contribute seven point five per cent each of employees ' total monthly emoluments olaide abass nta news

electronic gadgets the nigerian government is denouncing the measures being taken against nigerians minister of information and communication professor dora akunyili says the united states has to to passengers from some selected countries on us terror list which includes nigeria this development no doubt has continued to generate reactions from both government and the people of nigeria who have condemned the us decision professor ogaba ochei a director of research nigerian institute of international affairs believes that there is no moral justification by the us to humiliate other nigerians over an isolated case involving one individual his argument stems from the fact that the bomb suspect was not trained in nigeria and received no support from any individual or institution in agency warned that if not properly used the artemicin-based combination therapy known as acts which has transformed treatment in recent years could become ineffective the second edition of the guidelines for the treatment of malaria provides evidence base recommendations for countries on both diagnostic and treatment the main changes in this edition from the first which was published in two thousand and six are the emphasis on testing before treatment and the addition of a new act to the list of recommended treatments the agency said the aim is to reduce the emergence and spread of drug resistance with better management of malaria helping to tackle other problems like childhood illness and child survival half of the world 's population is at risk of contracting malaria every year there are two hundred and fifty million new cases resulting in eight hundred and sixty thousand deaths the majority of which are among african children it also affects fifteen million pregnant women accused at hilton hotel on december fifteenth to the death of chief bola ige on december twenty third two thousand and one idigbe submitted that to link reception to death will involve making a finding of fact based on speculation he explained that the police officer john ehigbaye stated that fryo never accepted the alleged five million naira placed on the killing of ige and that the accused never accepted fryo 's alleged counter offer of fifteen million naira stressing that this evidence was consistent with that of omisore going by the evidence given by fryo that they should ask omisore and abuja to state what they knew about the killing of chief bola ige idigbe said the accused did testify that he did not have access to additional security as the then deputy governor let alone having connection with abuja the defence council prayed court to see this evidence as an opinion evidence not establishing guilt he also submitted that since felix keyamo did not say that he saw fryo on the eleventh of january two thousand and two then the court should presumed that the oral testimony given by keyamo that he saw fryo on friday the twelfth of january was an afterthought testimony he therefore urged the court to give effect to the result of the polygraph test which he said is the only scientific and independent material that can resolve the contradiction between the statement of fryo and keyamo the hearing continues abdulrauf kamardeen nta news the united arab emirates health foundation award was jointly presented to the first lady mrs stella obasanjo and shaukat khanum memorial cancer hospital and research centre in pakistan for their outstanding contribution to health development the president of the world health assembly mister mohammed kahn says that the first lady through her pet project has contributed to the realisation of the national programme on immunisation aimed at reducing infant mortality and promoting safe motherhood in nigeria he said healthcare trusts have also initiated several other programmes aimed at improving education health and the rights of the child especially the more vulnerable the first lady mrs stella obasanjo could not hide her especially on the health and well-being of challenged children she said much of this work could be made easier and achievable if government and child-friendly organisations collaborate more to develop environment day it is the day people are called upon to maintain a healthy and clean environment environment day celebration started in nineteen seventy two after a resolution made at the united nations conference on the problem of human environment held in stockholm sweden various themes were selected over the years depending on environmental problems in the world at each given period they served as awareness for people to know the implication of polluting the earth this year 's theme wanted seas and oceans dead or alive calls on every geographical location colour race and social status to protect the precious gifts of nature the seas and oceans need adequate care as it is a major source of life and livelihood professor solomon fagade is an environmental specialist with industries on the increase due to economic development professor fagade speaks on measures that can be taken by the industries as well as individuals to maintain a hazard-free environment it has however been observed that it is not just the seas and oceans that need prompt and adequate attention but the entire environment since life one lives depends largely on one 's environment streets roads homes and market places should as well be kept clean and healthy to avert diseases kemi akinwande nta news organised by the centre for environmental protection and natural resources the lecture provides a meeting point for stakeholders in the environment on topical issues it is also to reflect on the nigerian environment take the impact of various activities and map out a strategy that will ensure the integration of sustainable environmental activities and the onerous task of employment generation rural wealth creation and national development professor rotimi oderinde represented the university of ibadan deputy chancellor academics hinted that there has been a general institutional weakness and lack of technical capacity to effectively tackle the nation 's environmental issues including threat to biological diversity the message of the united nation 's secretary general kofi annan for this year 's world environment day was delivered by professor sunday ajayi in it he noted that the marine environment is facing challenges that if not addressed immediately and effectively will have profound implications for sustainable development doctor kofi annan maintained that this year 's theme wanted seas and oceans dead or alive emphasised that society can no longer view the world 's seas as a communal dumping ground for our waste or as an unlimited resource of plenty tunji adepoju nta resolution of the twelfth general assembly of the international council of museum icom meeting in moscow in nineteen seventy seven the eighteenth day of may every year is therefore set aside as a special day when all museums in the world are recognised for their contributions to the modern world and also emphasise the universality and museum development in the growth of world culture delivering the lecture on this year 's theme museums and intangible heritage professor dele layiwola of institute of african studies university of ibadan said intangible heritage comprises literatures gege agbeni market is one filled with debris from one end to the other over time the place has come to be known for its dirt and traders going about their business despite the hazard it poses on their health a trader miss kafayat explained that two people were assigned by the community to watch over the bridge and prevent people from dumping refuse into the river this she said was effective until about five years ago when the two people no longer came around to guide and inspect the place she added that traders made efforts to stop people from throwing refuse over the bridge into the water channel but yielded no result another trader said the practice has been on for years she called on the government to help provide dustbins in the area and also clear blocked drainages in the environment especially now that the rains are here kemi akinwande nta news market survey showed that one plastic of rice was sold for between one hundred and thirty naira and one hundred and forty naira about a week ago now sells for between one hundred and sixty and one hundred and seventy naira a bag of rice which sold for four thousand two hundred naira last week now costs five thousand naira a trader explained that the ban on importation of rice is a major reason for its price hike she added that the non-availability of local rice in the market also contributes to the increase in price some traders noted that some people buy local rice straight from farmers in the northern parts of the country hence preventing its sufficient circulation another trader while appealing to the government to make local rice available in the market said the few bags available are so expensive a buyer also complained of the non-availability of local rice in the market she appealed to concerned authorities to encourage farmers in planting and processing local rice to make it readily available kemi akinwande nta news communication can be used as an instrument of informing entertaining and welcome back as part of the independence anniversary president goodluck jonathan last night conferred the golden honours award on eminent personalities past and present the president will later today address the nation and also renew the independence celebration forty six years of colonial rule came to an end on october first nineteen sixty when the union jack was lowered and replaced by the green white green of the new nation of nigeria as an independent country however the attainment of nigeria 's independence did not come without debates disagreements and agreements at conferences in this report hawa yusuf examines the constitutional conferences that led to the exit of colonial administration in nigeria now a quick look at the weather picture for abuja and other parts of the country that 's it on am news and congratulations to nigeria on nigeria 's fiftieth independence anniversary and for continuation of am express back to bilikisu in abuja this is lancaster house london the building that served as a venue for the series of constitutional conferences led into nigeria 's independence from nineteen fifty seven to nineteen sixty there were so many of such conferences the first took place in ibadan which was strongly criticised because of lack of consultation was decided that full consultation should be made to review the procedure however that constitution did not last therefore debates moved to the united kingdom in nineteen fifty seven which was attended by all political parties and chaired by the secretary of state for colonies mister linus boyd the final nigerian constitutional conference under the colonial administration opened in london from may ten to may nineteen nineteen sixty which authorised a committee of legal draftsmen to submit a draft of the independence constitution for approval the queen of england accepted and granted a full independence to nigeria on october one nineteen sixty a journey of eight hours on the train took us to shrewsbury a countryside in the united kingdom where we traced eileen samford an eighty seven year old woman whose husband sir hughes samford worked in nigeria during the colonial era and after the independence and eighty three year old professor lailaji baum was a lecturer at the university of ibadan abu and the university of lagos in the nineteen sixties and nineteen seventies both women took time to take us round their collections in nigeria and books they published on nigeria 's history whose proceeds they donate to charity organisation as well as sponsor adopted nigerian children from london i am hawa yusuf

the rule of law commitment to maintaining law and order now these two i realise are platforms on which you can build you know a developing nation without political stability you can not develop a us the opportunity to put in place the necessary reformages and legislative changes that are required to reposition our electoral process to provide it with this element of credibility necessary to ensure a stable political culture and a sustainable democracy now if you have this political stability arising out of this mergers then what is required is respect for the rule of law what we have linguistics is the is a discipline that deals with the analysis of all languages consequently language is prior to its analysis because if you do n't have a language what do you analyse this analysis is this analysis is done through the various branches of linguistics phonology morphology syntax semantic discourse analysis and any other linguistic branches that are yet to surface from time to time like science from time to time like science old theories of linguistics give room to new ones in the light of new discoveries linguistic theories continue to be chameleonic perennially creating problems for students who have to comprehend the old ones and at the same time grapple with the new theories they are ill equipped to understand a yoruba linguist of his worth ought to be well grounded in orature literature and linguistic nuances in order to capture the nitty-gritty of his analysis some people are doing this with masterly manipulation and the comprehension since linguistics in as- is an applied discipline the study of teaching of yoruba grammar is also a discipline on its own the function of a word in a sentence is erm in a or in a discourse has to be spelled out either as a noun a pronoun a verb an adverb preposition postposition interrogation gender marker tense marker an adjective interjection a conjunction noun number noun possessive case phrases sentences et cetera the study of yoruba language has to understand all this also in yoruba therefore the study of yoruba language is elaborate but it is not impossible because it is kept alive in orature and partially in literature its growth and life are guaranteed the study of literature is the study of the alphabetic cr- erm alphabetic creativities of writers in particular language in a particular language in all the various literary genres of the language and because human experiences are divergent from time to time from day to day from era to era and most especially from person to person orature literature can be sustained perennially except the human community is obliterated from the face of the earth or the literary tradition is gradually killed by lack of patronage but thank god people are still patronising the language introduction to yoruba language will not be complete without saying something on indigenous theory for the analysis of orature and literature of the language t m ilésanmí two thousand and four treated this topic extensively in his book yoruba orature and literature a cultural analysis this one you can find it in the bookshop olusola ajigbade two thousand and nine in his recent book published in germany bayreuth i think yes has popularised one of my cultural theories the binary theory of complementarity he writes this hypothesis of binary complimentarities was first introduced and used by ilésanmí in nineteen eighty nine to examine the literature of ifa among the yoruba people he postulates that the language of ifa shows the yoruba people or principle of philosophy of binary complementarities i 'm proud of this erudite scholar whose phd thesis i supervised some years before i retired at the age of sixty five years in this university in two thousand and five as long as those as long as those who are not tired are still writing and those in active service are not academically redundant there will be very vibrant oratoral and literary legacy left behind for the future generations now let us move to culture we have been talking about the language all through yoruba culture we have long desisted from looking at culture from its enticing flowery beauty we go in radice radice is a latin word in radice to the root and then move up to the flower the fruit of the yoruba culture gone are the days when we talk of culture as the exhibition of the dresses which the people wear their tribal marks the policies of the oba the various ceremonies the naming and the marital ritualistic ceremonies and some ephemeral tangent aspects of the core centre of culture we want to take our culture from the holistic point of view all cultures etymologically have their root in cult i repeat all cultures etymologically have their root in cult cult is defined as the preferential treatment of one or more things to the exclusion of others cult is a preferential treatment of one or more things to the exclusion of others t m ilésanmí two thousand and four page fifteen this special treatment which exclusively monopolises and denies to others is the essence of cult the idea of exclusiveness is embedded in the notion of cult and culture i have taught and even written extensively on this notion of cult on my work two thousand and four pages six to seventeen twenty nine to sixty four it suffices here to show the hierarchy of each of the twelve branches of culture in order to give us a brief but holistic view of culture here we shall also dive into practical presentation let us look at the chart of culture it is a paradigm for all cultures you can pass this after looking at it there are twelve disciplines here please when you will have looked at it you can pass it this way twelve disciplines but they are interconnected and interrelated anybody who treats culture without treating this twelve disciplines is only partially treating culture what are the twelve disciplines number one is the language each culture worth its name must be identified by a language either living or dead there is no culture without a language each miscegenated people or person speak one language or the other they may even speak many languages however if a language identifies a culture the person who speaks the language may be culturally ignorant of the essential ingredients of the culture that owns the language there are people who speak german without really knowing german culture but let us realise that the language is the inroad to culture number two is in the centre look at the centre governance it will reach you number two is governance it dictates the focus of the culture and maintains the equilibrium of the community the rise and the fall of each community are based on the nature of its central governance if those who control the culture the governors kept in the centre controls such a culture in a way that does n't allow the culture to develop they will not have a well developed culture the people whom they govern would still be regarded as non-developed community there are six groups of disciplines which are used either to goad or to cajole or to coax the community to behave one way or the other i will mention the six now myth religion fine art orature or literature law and defence this six disciplines of culture are used to goad or cajole or coax the community to have o- to behave one way or the other when a community starts what they would start with will be a myth they will talk about a deity bringing them to that land they may say they came from heaven or they came from far far far east that far far far east has no location really or far far far west and when we came to this land this person was ruling us and this person said this is what happened by the time they came to this land all those are myths it is on that myth that religion is based then they say our forefathers said they should be worshipped in this way or that way or this is the way we should worship god and a latin proverb again maybe because i 'm a priest cuius regio eius religio whosoever rules his religion prevails just look everywhere in the world whosoever rules his religion is popularised and is carried above other religions so religion is meant to direct people without any law but fulfilling the will of god and it makes them people it make the people obey their leaders much more than the law the leaders made so many people can die for their faith and they can not die for their nation that is the power of religion fine art can also be used to cajole to coax and erm also to goad the community ask me why or how for example everybody has a house but the house of the oba must be special it should not be called a house it is called a palace i wear a cap professor adegbite wears his own cap but an oba also has his own cap the oba 's own is not called a cap it 's called a crown but everything is designed by fine artists tailors erm asidelere those who use beads in making the crown everybody has got a husband or a wife the oba is said to have not a wife but olori no other person will call herself olori except the wife of the oba and then the olori must dress in a particular way created by the fine artists and erm they now see osun as the example of decorative erm fine artistry

must talk about language in general every language has oratural beginning oratural origin and even to date there is no language without orature when you speak german you are dealing with the ora- orature of german or when you write it you are dealing with the literature of german very few languages are reduced to alphabetic recording while the vice- vast majority remain orally spoken chanted or sung the yoruba language had the privilege of both orality and alphabetical recording in each of these two forms genres were developed that facilitated the growth of the language the oral genres include recitations chants and songs the recitations are brought alive in all forms of speech modes poetic r- recitations in oríkì incantations prayers divinations narrations of stories blessings and curses all these are presented as recitations the chanting mode of heightened rendition and freely prolonged vowels while the songs are rendered with strict regulated vowel lengthening with concomitant choreographic steps an example will buttress the above contention

literature and the development of our common humanity africa is experiencing a wave of enlightenment the kind the western world started experiencing since the eighteenth century the provision of computer and international communication network bring to the doorstep of whoever cares the latest discoveries in science and technology as well as the latest procedures in politics and religion the age of their discoveries made popular by the newton mathematical principles of natural philosophy is important in literature because of the revelation that the universe is not after all a mystery moving at the whim of an inscrutable god but on mechanism operating by a rational formula that can be understood by any rational man or woman men of this age sought order and found it in science but in spite of the attractions of science life remains poisoned at the font we may be joyful on the outside but we 're always aware of an inward groaning that produces tension nobody goes through the activities of the day indifferent to the pains of hunger and starvation ravages of war and accident the threats of armed robbery and kidnapping the danger of assassination and vip food poisoning and of course the uncertainties of monthly wages in all ours i- an age is an age of mixed emotions the political utopia of health for all by the year two thousand education for all by the year two thousand and food for all by the year two thousand is over and the perfect socio-political system where all live in peace and joy is after all a forlorn hope the image of the enslaver remains a strong the writer the writer 's creative imagination and energy are directed towards the analysis of the social political contradictions of the time communication between the old and the young generations of nigeria has also entered a crisis as in chimamanda 's purple hibiscus the elders no longer understand the youth and the youths can no longer tolerate the inconsistencies of unfulfiled promises also masses of the injustice and cruelty to which they are victims the poor masses for the poor masses what we experience is just like what akwanya described as the career of rough beasts there is no doubt like odili who would want the political system of their nation to conform to the rigid demands of absolute moral values many have therefore entered the field of politics with the aim of making themselves what is popularily referred to as a showcase of public morality but in most cases both the demands of the public whose interest these messiahs want to protect and the crushing weight of opposition from entrenched giants forced these messiahs to modify their principles of political morality the question of individuals and their lives including the confrontation of the social political problems has engaged literary literary voices have been heard with new levels of commitment and sophistication at the centre of society and the changing systems therefore is the artist who strives to capture and account for the socio-political and cultural events and changes and how they affect the lives of people and in doing this these artists exploit the linguistic resources at their disposal since language is that special mechanism for information storage and conveyance this language is always manipulated to suit the creative needs of the artist language and literature when w- harold in a paper i delivered last year to the university of nigeria nsukka chapter of the national association of students of english and literary studies i argued that the claim of superiority with the human species makes over other animals find its justification on its use of language first even though standing on two legs maybe one of the variables that distinguish a human being from other animals it is never enough after all some wild beasts like chimpanzees do stand on two legs but we refrain from calling them humans but any being who is capable of weaving words together who is capable of uttering them this in a sequential manner immediately lays an uncontrovertible claim to being human language therefore is the proof of the rationality of the rational animal from this point we can stretch our argument further by saying that the limits of language ?
therefore the limits of thought language is often seen as one of the important variables of culture one of the major philosophers of language in the twentieth century ludwig wittgenstein defines languages as a human feature that pictures facts i regard facts as environmental data in this sense we confirm that language pictures the environmental data and communicates same the environmental data do not present themselves in the same manner and at the same time to all people and every people try to find ways that are appropriate to them in order to capture environmental data what i 've been trying to say is that the env- the environment determines our languages in actual sense every language arose as a mechanism of problem solving for the people who invented it it is a matter of experience that most of these problems are peculiar to certain people who to make sense of their peculiar experience must invent words both for the problems and their solutions this gives you a clue why some words with their meanings are found in one language and are not found in another as yet no nigerian language has any word to capture the reality of hurricanes which have been devastating parts of america in recent times cultural literacy one of the shoestrings of knowledge which are still retained quite vividly from my elementary school days is the definition of culture as the totality of a people 's way of life today i know that my elementary school definition is a slight modification of edward taylor definition of culture and that this totality of people 's way of life includes their acquired language beliefs arts law morals customs habits and a host of material objects culture portrays our shortcomings and our strengths as human beings at birth the human being is the only animal that is not naturally adapted to the environment even the basest of all animals can find its way around the world and on its own withstand the harsh conditions of life on earth at birth an newly born goat that is the kid can instinctively sense danger and flee from it right from the day of its birth but it is not so with a human infant at birth it is so disadvantaged to the extent that other animals seem more developed than it it is this that are- it is in this that our shortcomings as cultural animals lie the strength of a human being as a cultural animal lies in the fact out of all ?
on multiculturalism william shakespeare used to see the world as a stage in my own view the world is a market place the greatness of a market is measured by the variety of goods and services available to buyers the beauty of the world and therefore of every human society consists in the availability of alternative ways of doing things cultural alternative is an enchantment and it is what we express in the terms cultural pluralism and multiculturalism a child newly introduced to the subject of mathematics is elated that there are many rules to eighteen than nine plus nine the sum of two and sixteen five and thirteen seven and eleven and so on will always give him eighteen first the existence of multiculture represents man 's various answers to the same question a people may value an answer which other people may consider nonsensical a story is told of a european christian who had gone to the cemetery to deposit a bunch of flower at the grave of a dead relation while she carried out her mission she noticed a chinese placing rice at the grave of his own dead relation the european considered the chinese insane and wondered how he thought that the dead relation would rise to eat the rice he placed at his graveside but then would the european 's relative have risen to smell the flower placed at her grave a serious thought reveals that there is no essential difference in their actions the only difference is that they exploit the sensing in different cultures by european value flowers while rice is of a special value to the chinese my proposition here is that the distinction that is made between cultures is more of what is valued in an area and not valued as such in another climate and environment have great roles to play here and the one of the paradoxes of our age is that globalisation threatens to impose on us a culture of sameness throughout the world this implies submission to cultural imperialism the information technology wants to turn the entire world into a homogeneous culture this is exemplified in the current worldwide campaign to civilise the uncivilised societies my grandmother in anambra who to keep to her two o'clock appointment measures the length of her shadow against the afternoon sun is regarded as primitive even though she has never been late to such meetings the cnn advertisement in the television constantly reminds her that she should own a watch if she desires her name to be erased from the list of primitive people some other people who claim to be more or less sensible than the cnn advertisers would even take arms against her because they consider her practice as great embarrassment to their own culture today we are at crossroads where proponents of cultural imperialism hold sway this trend began to be explicit seeing the works of karl marx and august kant who saw society as an evolutionary unit marx 's case particularly explains the complexity of the human mind as the same marx whose writings were against political and economic imperialism promoting the same kind of imperialism cultural imperialism we are now conversant with the derogatory classification of some societies as primitive and therefore bad in the same vein other societies are seen as scientific and therefore good the same trend is followed in the classification of some societies as developed and others as non-developed some as civilised and others as non-civilised this way of classification ?
of mesopotamia babylon and ionia first taught the world the games of multiculturalism the early trader did not despise the culture of other people he admired it because it either produced or created the need for what he sold i tend to think that if the trader was not open to other cultures the cross-cultural fertilisation that is the germ of modern civilisation would not have taken place nathan glazer in his book of nineteen ninety seven entitled we are all multiculturalists now not minding the gains made by globalisation argues that in today 's world there has gone ahead institutionalisation of ethnic and ra- and racial differences so that multiculturalism has been instu- inst- institutionalised as an ideology put concretely as i understand it multiculturalism insists that i do not do away with my igboness in order to be a true nigerian but more concretely i do not have to force the urhobo student in my department to forget her urhoboness and adopt my igboness i have just started a tradition in my university it is a personal rebellion against any sort of cultural imposition as a rule i now attend my friday lectures in my igbo traditional attire colleagues have always commended me in such attires and i used to wonder how colourful the university can look if the yoruba lecturer also appears in his own native wear ditto the idoma and all others language use in a multicultural nigeria ?
benedict gave a definition of culture as that which binds men together she may be right as some of the men written about present the same culture in intercultural relations culture is often seen as that which pulls men apart samuel huntington captures this fact well when he observes that culture often clash in rwanda as in many other parts of africa the world witnessed horrible scenes of homicide as a result of the tendency of cultures to clash and pull people apart in nigeria there is constant uprising occasioned by our inability to condone cultural differences of all the elements of culture language is the only one capable of expressing cultural differences juergen habermas distinguishes between a strategic use of language and a genuinely communicative use of language in a genuinely communicative use of language the language speaker intends to speak out to play the primordial role assigned to language to communicate and to inform here what is communicated is the fact in a strategic use of language the speaker intends to achieve purposes other than communication in intercultural relation a strategic user of language may decide to use his language to disparage other language users that is people of other cultures first he begins to denigrate such people and their culture as inferior second he starts to convert them into his own culture third when convers- when conversion fails he marks them out for elimination we may yet recall to mind the strategic use of language ?
sometimes in ?
where the hutu broadcaster referred to the tutsis as cockroaches a spontaneous massacre of the tutsi which his speech generated is a testimony of the harm strategic use of language can cause in a society ladies and gentlemen i have earlier congratulated myself for the composition of my audience which comprises of university people a university person is often assumed to be a person of culture we take a person of culture to mean one who can speak languages other than his own who can do things other than in the ways his own people do them a cultured person does not offend the sensibilities of the other person from the other culture among other things he is careful about the words he uses to refer to what a person or other culture does in my part of the country for instance the bride price must be paid before a girl fully assumes role as wife to a man i have heard that no money is involved among the yoruba but among the fulani i was told a man must prove himself by withstanding many strokes of the cane before he takes a woman home as his wife the various practices cited above may seem bizarre to a person alien towards the particular culture where that is practised but it will amount to strategic use of language if one begins to taunt the igbo because they collect bride price as engaging in woman trade that is selling the bride such use of language does not affect national integration and unity positively my conclusion let me start by telling you of my observation i see the manner language is used as the cause of the recurrent interethnic crisis that we witness in nigeria today a judicious use of language demand that i should be sensitive to the cultural realities of a other people the ijaw should be sensitive to the cultural realities of the yoruba and yoruba to the jukun and jukun to the idoma and so on i have earlier observed that the making of the nigeria of the next fifty years belongs to all of you my dear students i inform you at once that a peaceful multicultural nigeria that is devoid of ethnic tribal and religious tensions is possible but we must work for such a nigeria if the need be we must take arms against every strategic use of language against any person anywhere in our country no such use is to be neglected for even the minutest of it is as damaging as the biggest one let me strike properly my point with a bedouin legend told by f- friedman about the origin of crisis in the middle east it is the story of an old bedouin man and his turkey one day an old beduin man discovered that his he could restore his virility by eating a turkey so he bought a small turkey and kept around his tent feeding it so that it would provide a source of renewed strength one day the turkey was stolen so the bedouin called his sons together and said boys my turkey has been stolen we are in danger now his sons laughed replying father it 's not big deal what do you need a turkey for never mind the father replied we must get the turkey back but his sons did n't take this seriously and soon forgot all about the turkey a few weeks later the bedouin sons came to him and said father our camel has been stolen what should we do find my turkey he responded a few weeks later the sons came to him again saying that the old man 's house was stolen find my turkey the bedouin replied finally a few weeks after that someone wed the bedouin 's daughter then he gazed at his sons and said it 's all because of the turkey when they saw that they could take away my turkey we lost everything the point i am making finally is that the assurance that he went free unchallenged after the strategic use of language will embolden a person to try it a second time then it will embolden everybody to try it always we can not guess the consequences indeed ladies and gentlemen you have been a wonderful audience i thank you for your listening ears i thank you more for your patience i thank you i thank you again and again
