in accordance with the alma-ata declaration on primary health care health is declared as a fundamental human right and the attainment of the highest possible level of health is the most important worldwide social goal this notwithstanding the budgetary allocation to the health sector has not in any way reflected this reality in nigeria yearly budgetary allocation to the health sector is less than six per cent of the total national budget as against the fifteen per cent recommended by the united nations and the abu- abuja declaration by african countries there is need for a stronger political will on the part of government to properly fund the health sector in the health sector there are many professional groups that team up or should team up to ensure that health which is expected to be a fundamental human right is a right indeed each of these professional groups has a distinct role to play in the provision of health care pharmacist is one of these health care professional trained to provide services that are promotive preventive curative and rehabilitative with medicine as the main instrument of intervention medicine give credibility to health cases and also serve as major determinant of health services utilisation it therefore stands to reason that substantive improvements in the supply and use of medicines are necessary for the overall improvement of the health status of the population in the use of medicines cost is a major factor because medicines could constitute as much as twenty per cent of total national health expenditures a most pressing issue is that at present in nigeria the laudable national health insurance scheme notwithstanding most consumers of medicines pay out of their pockets most leading causes of death and disability in developing countries can be prevented treated or at least alleviated with cost-effective essential medicines despite this fact literally hundreds of millions of people do not have regular access to essential medicines many of those who do have access are given the wrong treatment or fake medicine receive too little medicines for their illness or do not use them correctly two point zero the national drug policy the maiden national drug policy for nigeria was adopted and launched in nineteen ninety against the background of inadequacies in medicine availability supply and distribution resulting from various factors the primary goal of the policy was to make available at all times to the nigerian populace adequate supplies of medicines that are effective affordable safe and of good quality to ensure the rational use of such medicines and to stimulate increased production of essential medicines in other words the national drug policy was established to ensure an effective and efficient medicine management system in the country the strategies for implementation were well articulated but unfortunately most of the set targets of the national drug policy have not been met the pharmacist is regarded as a medicine expert and if there is going to be any positive change in medicine management pharmacists are best positioned to ensure this it is in this context that i have focussed on promoting effective and efficient management of medicine use and supply as my research and service efforts in the last two decades three point zero the tree model i have taken a holistic approach in trying to present a model that captures most of what is needed for effective and efficient management of medicine use and supply in nigeria figure one shows the tree model some components of which will be the focus of this inaugural lecture the tree model pictorially presents the features of the medicine use and supply system that 's effective and efficient resulting in rational dispensing rational prescribing appropriate use of medicines by patients available and affordable safe and effective essential medicines as well as value for money all of which will lead to better health for all these are the fruits the nutrients useful and toxic that the plant feeds on include contributions of open markets consumers health care professionals finance institutions electronic and print media schools and universities pharmaceutical industry health facilities itinerant vendors and the government the nutrients are channelled through the trunk without which there would be no fruits the trunk consists of legislation and regulation human resource development medicine selection process supply management quality assurance rational drug use as well as monitoring and evaluation the components of the tree model have been my main main interest in the last thirty years and will remain in my focus until i retire by god 's grace from the university academic activity four point zero the making of a pharmacist four point one pharmacy curriculum pharmacy is a science that should respond positively to the social cultural and economic aspects of human activities in its quest to promote the use of medicine in safe quantities to conquer disease and disease states with minimum discomfort to the users the way a pharmacist will practise his profession is a product of training skills acquired and ability for self-development over the years we have been studying the curricula of pharmacy faculties in nigerian universities with the main aim of identifying weaknesses and suggesting how best these deficiencies could be corrected we had looked at pharmacy curricula of eighteen different countries and came to the conclusion that there is no model professional pharmacy curriculum for all nations what we need is a nigerian pharmaceutical education system deriving its strength from the present and future healthcare needs of the nigerian people and gaining international recognition and credibility basically through the quality of the research output of pharmacy academia pharmaceutical education in nigeria has its roots in the british educational system and has undergone a lot of positive changes over the years it moved from two to three-year diploma in the nineteen thir- fifties to three to four-year degree programme in the nineteen sixties from one institution for the training of pharmacists in the nineteen fifties to over ten accredited degree awarding faculties by twenty ten pharmacy practice has moved from focussing on the product to the patient it is expected that good quality pharmaceutical education must produce a graduate who is technically competent scholastically capable socially relevant and morally disciplined in the passage of time we have faced the challenge of narrowing the gap between training and practice the training of the pharmacist is moving into a new level with the introduction of the six-year doctor of pharmacy degree programme in nigerian universities this is a positive response to the global community expectation of a pharmacist trained to provide pharmaceutical care and relevant in delivering the emerging responsibilities of the pharmacist this programme should not be seen in the context of titles but service the goal of the pharmd programme is to produce pharmacy practitioners with knowledge skills and motivation to provide comprehensive pharmaceutical services i fully believe that this is the way to go for the profession i was the foundation dean of the faculty of pharmacy niger delta university wilberforce island bayelsa state nigeria i was also consultant to the delta state university assisting the pioneer dean of the faculty of pharmacy professor david okpako to develop their pharmacy programme in both capacities i favoured the pharmd programme here in ife as the premier pharmacy faculty in nigeria we must rise up to the challenge of the implementation of pharmd programme if we are to remain relevant the level of facilities available in the faculty is very important as we consider taking this positive step i recall how our faculty here in ife that runs a bpharm programme was granted interim accreditation status some years ago because of the absence of a pharmacy information centre and a clinical pharmacy programme that was considered rather deficient in clerkship exposure by the accreditation panel i was appointed acting head of the culprit department right in the face of the challenge of interim accreditation in two thousand and two we could not get university funds to establish the pic i initiated a process that resulted in my department floating a national workshop the proceeds from that workshop was used to establish a pharmacy information centre and refurbish to date our pharmacy practice laboratory there is no big nigerian university with better facilities to date we are working hard on making the services of the pic more relevant to the university community in addition to its important role in the training of pharmacy students four point two the pharmacy lecturer the brain drain phenomenon began biting hard in pharmacy many years ago we are still finding it difficult to attract our best graduating students to take up jobs as demonstrators in the in the faculty this is attributed to the poor salaries of academic pharmacists relative to their hospital counterparts the recent salary increase has not significantly changed the situation the telecom industry has remained a main attraction for pharmacists while this suggests the robustness of the training of the pharmacist as a versatile graduate the trend calls for concern the challenge of brain drain prompted us to study how academic pharmacists responded to the relatively poor salaries in nigerian universities the study identified about eighteen different types of individual coping strategies sources of extra income identified by respondents varied ranging from academic non-academic to social support in the prevailing adverse socio-economic environment it is feasible to create conditions that allow individual pharmacy lecturers adopt strategies that are compatible with equity and quality by responding to their aspiration for survival social status and professional satisfaction one practical way of attracting and retaining pharmacist lecturers is to allow academic pharmacists register pharmaceutical premises for practice this will not only improve their income base and check economically catalysed brain drain but will most importantly provide lecturers with relevant practical examples to illustrate their lectures this is necessary if lecturers are to promote entrepreneurship in pharmacy if you do not possess or acquire the entrepreneurial spirit you can not impart entrepreneurship four point three teaching of pharmacy students when a fresh lecturer is appointed it is assumed that the academic qualification is adequate to make a person effective as a teacher my over thirty years experience as a lecturer has shown me that this is not true when i joined the services of this university as an assistant lecturer in nineteen eighty i initiated the use of the student evaluation of teaching questionnaire which i obtained from the university the obafemi awolowo university senate paper after my lectures questionnaires were distributed to all students and analysed thereafter i did this for three consecutive years the reports were sent to the head of department through internal memorandum of third april nineteen eighty one and eighteenth march nineteen eighty two seventh april and at twelfth april nineteen eighty three in fact by a memo of fifteenth of april nineteen eighty one the head of department professor abayomi sofowora acknowledged my first memo with these remarks and i quote i like this kind of initiative on your part to assess yourself end of quote i hold a strong position that the most reliable means of determining whether or not a lecturer is effective is through student evaluation not necessarily examination performance over the years the comments of students on my teaching has helped me to sharpen my teaching skills significantly it is therefore not surprising that when the federal ministry of health in collaboration with world health organisation organised a workshop in october two thousand and five for lecturers in pharmacy to generate and share information on contemporary pharmacy practice issues in order to evolve a modern curriculum that will be responsive to current challenges in pharmacy practice i was one of the three lecturers in nigeria selected as resource persons there is a need for all fresh lecturers to be exposed to basic teaching techniques when they 're employed this should be institutionalised communication skills do not necessarily improve with progression on the academic ladder mister vice chancellor when i was head of department all newly appointed lecturers were made to attend my lectures as observers after the lectures i welcomed their comments through which i also trained them on how best to teach pharmacy students i also attended some of their lectures to further guide them this is an initiative a form of mentoring that could be formalised we as pharmacy lecturers need to be effective communicators to produce the kind of pharmacists that can drive a model management system for medicine use and supply four point four the pharmacy students the undergraduate pharmacy programme has always been regarded as very hectic in the nineteen eighties pharmacy students hardly belonged to socio-cultural organisations as a result of lack of time and interest results from one of our studies prompted an awareness creation to encourage pharmacy students to be more interested in social cultural and religious activities the outcome is that pharmacy students are becoming more involved in such activities lecture attendance is a vital aspect of the assessment of the pharmacy student active participation at lectures will be affected by various factors including motivation we have shown that attitude teaching style and competence of lecturers affect students ' motivation for class attendance in addition to the structuring of lecture timetable and the adequacy of available facilities the above factors should therefore be considered in the process of curriculum development and implementation interpersonal communication with patients is a key factor in managing service encounter this affects this affects building lasting relationships with patients we carried out a study to assist pharmacy educators assess the need or otherwise for the development of an appropriate interpersonal skill training course that will assist in reducing communication apprehension amongst pharmacy students i am happy to note that communication skill training is now a composite part for the pharmacy curriculum in nigerian pharmacy schools five point zero the practice of pharmacy the practice of pharmacy takes place in academic hospital industrial and community setting the challenges faced in each of these career options vary however hospital and community pharmacist are the most visible in the pharmacy profession five point one hospital pharmacy my major interest has been the empowering of hospital pharmacists to improve upon the quality of pharmaceutical services provided in public health in- institutions in nigeria hospital pharmacy practice is fairly well established in government-owned secondary and tertiary healthcare institutions in nigeria this is not the case with private healthcare institutions we sought to examine the role played by pharmacists in private clinics and the physician 's perception of pharmacists ' role about sixty per cent of the sixty four private clinics studied had pharmacy and forty eight per cent of these had resident pharmacist the study showed that the level of record keeping of medicines in the private clinics was generally very low where there were no pharmacist this has implications for the appropriate monitoring of the therapeutic efficacy of medicine especially where there is a need for change in dosage regimen the study showed that the level of pharmacy practice in private clinics in nigeria is far from satisfactory it is set in the national drug policy it is specified in section six sixteen two subsection three that and i quote in every health care facility where there is a qualified and licensed medical practitioner there shall be a qualified and licensed pharmacist to manage medicines end of quote this provision should be enforced there are too many private clinics and hospitals that operate without pharmacists the level of confidence the patient has in the patient could affect his level of compliance with dispensed medication we carried out a study on how patients perceived the services rendered by the pharmacy in a public hospital the study showed a correlation between the time patients spent on collecting medicines politeness of the pharmacy staff and the rating of the performance of the pharmacist this led us to study patients ' response to waiting time we found that the total waiting time for a dispensing process averaged seventeen minutes and ninety per cent of this was due to delay component we therefore recommended that more time should be devoted to counselling while reducing the total time spent by the patient in having their prescription script erm sheet filled it is gratifying to note that the time spent on prescription billing and payment has improved significantly at the obafemi awolowo university teaching hospital complex as an outcome of this study five point one point one drug revolving fund the drug revolving fund is a fund for which capital is provided by government appropriation or bilateral donation but which is intended to be self -- sustaining with all expenditures being replaced from money collected from the fund this is a cost recovery mechanism experiences have shown that although the concept of drf is simple the successful e- establishment and long-term sustainability of such schemes have been fraught with difficulty often the monies collected are insufficient to replenish the original stocks and the fund gradually gets depleted the drf has assisted in ensuring sustainable medicine supply in many public health institutions in nigeria in fact the obafemi awolowo university hospitals complex is one of the drf success stories in nigeria nationally the major challenges facing the scheme is the feeling of many hospital chief executives that the drf fund is money available for emergency use this situation is worsening with the current economic meltdown and i 've advocated over the years consistent with the provisions of the essential drug programme regulations that the head of a hospital pharmacy department should be a signatory to the drf account in this way it would be easier to separate surplus fund from the monies needed to replenish stock thereby preventing decapitalisation financial discipline rather than a one-time capital grant is the key to a successful drf there are so many suppliers who will be ready to supply large quantities of medicines on credit provided that the hospital will keep to agreed payment terms i venture to say that with drf the question of out of stock medicines should be alien to us may i also say that funding should not be a challenge in the financing of medicine supply in the public sector because there is ready market what the governments need to do is to go into partnership with reputable manufacturers an importers with binding contractual agreements and memoranda of understanding that will make such suppliers supply medicines on mutually agreed credit terms this will also curtail the challenge of drugs expiring on the shelves in some hospital five point one point two unit dose dispensing system the unit dose dispensing system udds for short is a system of distributing medications to inpatients in the hospital in which each patient 's medication requirements are given for only twenty four hours on unit dose dispensing of medication is a standard practice in hospitals in developed countries a major advantage of udds is that it brings pharmacists into the medication use process in order to reduce medication error and enhance the quality of pharmaceutical care to the patient udds has many advantages over the traditional system of dispensing these advantages include improved patient care reduction in medication errors more efficient use of hospital personnel significantly reduced medicine cost alleviation of medicine shortages ready availability of medicine information to the other health professionals and patients and better relationship among the health professionals in addition to accurate determination of medicine cost the earliest attempt to introduce and operate udds in nigeria was at the beginning of the nineteen eighties but it was not quite successful in nineteen ninety eight national agency for food and drug administration and control passed a circular urging all the teaching hospitals to adopt the system so as to incorporate all the advantages of the system into their work nothing came out of the circular in fact all efforts to adopt the udds proved abortive however in two thousand and one at the curriculum review committee conference organised by the pharmacists council of nigeria in sango ota where i was invited to make an expert presentation on pharmacy administration i interacted with pharmacist obiaga who initiated the scheme at the ahamdu bello university teaching hospital in the nineteen eighties he narrated the problems being faced in the implementation of the system nationwide i told him that i will successfully midwife the project thereafter i initiated a series of national training workshops on udds the first of these workshops was held in this university in two thousand and two this was followed by sensitisation through mobilisation workshops for nurses as well as medical doctors in the last couple of years i have been engaged in the training of hospital pharmacists across the country on unit dose dispensing system in march two thousand and five a state wide implementation workshop on the udds sponsored by the world bank assisted health system development project was organised by the akwa ibom state government thereafter it was adopted as a statewide scheme expectedly pharmacist doctor obiaga the father of udds in nigeria and my humble self the mother of udds in nigeria were lead resource persons at the historic workshop it is quite heart warming to note that a good number of hospitals in nigeria have adopted the system thereby drawing from its tremendous benefits i was the consultant appointed to implement the udds at university college hospital ibadan i consider the udds as one of my major contributions to the development of hospital pharmacy administration in nigeria five point two community pharmacy community pharmacy practice is essentially retail pharmacy business any retail medicine outlet with a displayed register pharmacy emblem is a community pharmacy it is only with the pharmacy emblem that you can physically differentiate a community pharmacy from a patent medicine store patent medicine stores are operated by non-pharmacists who are holders of patent and proprietary medicine vendor 's licenses the medicines sold in patent medicine stores are restricted in practice however many patent medicine stores operate as pharmacies clinics or even as hospitals the relevant regulatory bodies could save some time from the overregulation of registered pharmaceutical premises which are superintended by qualified and registered pharmacists so that they could better control the operations in patent medicine stores it is my considered opinion that registered community pharmacies could play the role of primary health care centres for the delivery of health services such as family planning immunisation and specialised medication counselling this was what prompted me to do a research proposal that secured a grant from the johns hopkins university population communication services of the united states agency for international development to carry out a nationwide survey on the role of pharmacists in family health the initial study determined the level of ?
of pharmacist 's knowledge of family planning methods as well as established the willingness of pharmacists to participate in family planning activities we also established that the family planning content of the undergraduate pharmacy curriculum was inadequate we made appropriate recommendations i dilated on this in my keynote address at the two thousand and eight pharmacy week of the ondo state branch of the pharmaceutical society of nigeria six point zero maintaining competence of pharmacists maintaining competence throughout their career is a life-long challenge for all health care professionals including pharmacists being aware of the fast evolution of knowledge and the responsibilities of health care professionals there are genuine concerns about maintaining competence this moral sense however but has not always sufficiently self-moti- motivated health care professionals to continuously pursue new knowledge consequently professional associations and authorities alike started developing formal life-long learning systems with the aim of sustaining the practitioner 's competence and ensuring the provision of quality professional services
this presentation is in continuation of the determination of the commission to give the country a transparent credible and acceptable census i would like to say erm what not many people do not appreciate and that is that census is next to war and worldwide as the biggest peacetime administrative activity in terms of mass mobilisation and mom- mobilisation of materials and according to the united nations guidelines it takes about thirty six months advance preparations to undertake a population and housing census therefore contrary to popular public erm opinion or perception census is not a touch-and-go affair it 's not an- something that you would decide e- you are going to do it today or tomorrow or in two weeks ' time in a month 's time or in a year 's time and it just happens tribute to my grandfather papa nnukwu when i heard of your exit of this sinful world it pained me so much this is because even though you were old sick and bedridden you were still able to recognise everyone that comes around you and could still engage such in a meaningful conversation it was indeed a delight talking with you well we can not question god who is all loyal papa we will indeed miss you you were a man of the people a peace lover a mentor to many a community leader an opinion moulder you lived a very sacrificial life as you would spend your last kobo to give relief to the trouble you were fondly called by those who love you and those who have benefitted from your benevolence emeriaku you were hard-working and a master of your work your mastery of mason work made you number one choice of every intending builder your devotion to the service of god is unequalled the church where you worshipped is many miles away but you never missed service for once no matter the weather situation or your health condition except when your feet could no longer carry you even then you continued to say your prayers without ceasing even in your sickbed mhm papa how can we handle the vacuum your exit has created in our hearts we have resolved to take consolation in the exemplary life you lived the legacy l- you left for us will be our watchword your values we will uphold your advices we will follow your dreams we will live out your achievements we will consolidate adieu papa you have gone but never forgotten your memories will ever remain fresh in our mind until we see again on that resurrection morning may the merciful lord grant your gentle soul eternal rest good night papa

eleven general elections became more complicated yesterday when the federal high court in lagos ruled that the nineteen ninety nine constitution has not been legally amended justice okechukwu okeke who had earlier tacitly ordered the national assembly not to proceed further with the task of amending the nineteen ninety nine constitution finally declared yesterday as legally inoperative or to use the proper legal terminology inchoate the judge declared as inchoate the constitution of the federal republic of nigeria first authorisation act twenty ten published as government notice number three oh two unless the president signs it justice okeke declared as null and void the constitution amended and will remain void until it is set to hold the president for his assent the judgement that the ninety nine constitution itself came into being through an act and therefore can only be amended through an act and once this is so an act of the national assembly can not become an act unless it fulfils all the rites of passage including that of the presidential assent in response to an argument or a postulation that amendments to the american constitution were not signed by the by american presidents justice okeke noted that the two constitutions do not have the same origin while the nigerian constitution came in through an act the american constitution on the other hand came in via a proclamation moreover there is a section fifty eight in the nigerian constitution which already outlined how an act of the national assembly can came ab- w- can come about now the reactions have been literary works if you did any work in soyinka or achebe you always find words that are not actually english but which the writer tried to use to carry the peculiar message that 's not wh- what i 'm saying what i am afraid of and what i want you to take note of none of us has the solution now is this uncontrolled use of text messaging and other forms of pseudo-journalism and for somebody who says like adegbite made mention yesterday that students most of our students can speak the language fluently but can not write perfectly if we are to capture the speeches of these students and analyse them you will see all kinds of errors there is a difference between rattling and speaking impeccable english what students do when they come together is to rattle sometimes you do n't even get at what they 're saying but if you were to capture what they 're saying and you place it to proper scrutiny you discover all kinds of errors but the point i am trying to make is that if we have to continue with this text messages abbreviations jargons that have no measure of control if we have to continue with free-for-all use of language you see all kinds of things even people who are stark illiterates in the use of language they just you know coin anything and use it for this messaging if we continue with that then we shall be in trouble in future and my solution as a person had asked is that students of english should try to control their text messages as far as possible to reflect the language you may use all forms of abbreviation but you do n't use abbreviations that carry and portray you as an illiterate you must use those words and abbreviations that will show that at least you have some measure of knowledge of the language that may be that may not be the actual solution but you we shall get to the point when we continue the way we are going you may discover that even in our formal writing essay writing students will just use anything of course it 's started even in my essays essays in my class students make use of anything everything and they think they 're writing essays in other words anybody who can not dis- distinguish between formal and informal usage has failed so it does n't matter how well you speak if you can not write according to the context and the formality of the situation or informality of the situation then you have failed then for the person who asked about
